one_line_takeaway: In a matched bilingual red-teaming study of four frontier MLLMs, switching adversarial prompts from English to Mexican Spanish attenuates linguistic/rhetorical jailbreak techniques but amplifies visual multimodal attacks, producing a model-safety rank reversal (Qwen Omni overtakes Pixtral Large as most vulnerable) that no scalar correction of English-only benchmarks can recover.
key_finding: Own-data results: (1) Model safety ranking overall (both harm severity ORs and observed ASR) is Claude Sonnet 4.5 (most resistant, 11.3% overall ASR) < GPT-5 (14.7%) < Qwen Omni (33.4%) < Pixtral Large (41.2% -- most vulnerable), with all pairwise contrasts credible (e.g., Pixtral vs Claude harm OR=9.61 [8.97,10.36]). (2) Generational comparison against Van Doren & Ford's predecessor models shows declining absolute ASR in three of four families: Pixtral Large 62.4%->41.2%, Qwen Omni 38.6%->33.4%, GPT-5 19.2%->14.7%, while Claude Sonnet is essentially flat (10.7%->11.3%), interpreted as having hit a floor for single-turn static attacks; within-family gains have not closed cross-model gaps. (3) The core dissociation: overall ASR is higher for en-US (27.8%) than es-MX (22.6%); Pixtral's ASR drops 50.5%->32.0% under Spanish (OR=0.38 [0.30,0.47]) and GPT-5's drops 18.1%->11.2% (OR=0.53 [0.42,0.67]); Claude shows no credible language difference (11.3% vs 11.4%). Qwen Omni is the exception: its ASR rises under Spanish (31.2%->35.6% [es-MX 35.6% overall in one table, 34.8%/36.4% by modality]), and es-MX raters rate Qwen Omni as more harmful than Pixtral (OR=1.29 [1.19,1.39]), reversing the en-US ordering (OR=0.70 [0.66,0.73] in the other direction) -- a rank reversal the authors argue no scalar correction can recover. (4) Attack-technique analysis: role-play is the only technique credibly more effective than the noise-adding reference (OR=3.14 [1.54,6.04]); ignore-instructions is credibly less effective than baseline (OR=0.41 [0.18,0.91]). All five primary linguistic techniques are credibly less effective in es-MX than en-US, largest attenuation for role-play (OR=0.45 [0.36,0.56]); conversely toxic-image execution is credibly MORE effective in es-MX (OR=1.58 [1.33,1.87]), completing the linguistic-attenuation/visual-amplification dissociation. (5) Modality effects are language-moderated: in en-US a credible modality effect appears only for Pixtral Large, while in es-MX modality effects are credible across all four models. (6) Prompt-level variance dominates model-identity variance (harm SD=1.80, ASR SD=1.74), meaning aggregate ASR is sensitive to benchmark composition.
core_contribution: The paper reports the first systematic cross-lingual, multimodal red-teaming evaluation of frontier MLLMs, comparing jailbreak vulnerability in US English (en-US) vs Mexican Spanish (es-MX) across four models: Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5, Pixtral Large, and Qwen Omni. Using a fixed benchmark of 363 adversarial prompt scenarios (from a prior study, Van Doren & Ford) administered in text-only and multimodal conditions in both languages, the authors collected 52,272 harm ratings and binary attack-success judgments from matched panels of nine native-speaker annotators per language. They fit Bayesian mixed-effects (cumulative-link and Bernoulli) models with crossed random effects for prompts and raters to decompose model, modality, and language effects. The central, own-data finding is a dissociation: linguistic/rhetorical jailbreak techniques (e.g., role-play) become credibly less effective under Spanish prompting, while visually explicit multimodal attacks become more effective, and this pattern causes a rank reversal in which Qwen Omni overtakes Pixtral Large as the most vulnerable model among es-MX participants -- a reversal the authors show cannot be recovered by any scalar correction of English-condition scores.
threat_model: Adversary submits single-turn adversarial prompts (text-only or multimodal: text+image) to commercial MLLM APIs, using six categories of jailbreak techniques (role-play/hypothetical persona, strategic framing/persuasion, refusal suppression, ignore-instructions, response priming, noise/distraction) and three multimodal execution methods (harmful text + harmless image; text embedded in image; harmful image + harmless/ambiguous caption), across three harm categories (illegal activities, disinformation, unethical behaviour). The novel axis introduced by this paper is prompt language (en-US vs es-MX, via professional human translation) as an independent variable interacting with modality and model identity; the paper does not introduce new attack techniques itself -- it reuses an existing validated benchmark (Van Doren & Ford, arXiv:2509.15478) and evaluates how the SAME attack surface shifts under a language switch.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluation was API-only with no visibility into training data, alignment procedures, or moderation layers, so behavioral differences cannot be attributed to specific architectural/tuning mechanisms; (2) the benchmark is a fixed set of 363 single-turn prompts across 3 harm categories -- multi-step, agentic, and multi-turn dynamics may expose qualitatively different vulnerabilities not captured; (3) multimodal prompts were limited to static images across 3 predefined execution strategies -- audio and dynamic cross-modal sequences may expose distinct failure modes not tested; (4) prompts were translated rather than natively authored by cultural insiders, so while the dissociation pattern is inconsistent with a uniform cultural-mismatch account, technique-specific prompt-side confounds cannot be fully excluded. Additionally, the study covers only one language pair (en-US/es-MX) and the authors explicitly flag that generalization to other language pairs, especially lower-resource ones, is an open question.

datasets_used: 363-scenario adversarial prompt benchmark reused from Van Doren & Ford (arXiv:2509.15478): 167 illegal-activity, 68 disinformation, 128 unethical-behaviour prompts, each in text-only and multimodal (text+image) versions (726 prompt versions per model), constructed by 26 experienced red teamers using six attack strategies (role-play n=127, strategic framing n=62, refusal suppression n=54, ignore-instructions n=41, response priming n=39, noise/distraction n=33, other n=7) and three multimodal execution methods (harmful text+harmless image n=211, text embedded in image n=89, harmful image+harmless caption n=63); professionally translated to Mexican Spanish by two native speakers with third-party accuracy review

52,272 harm-rating/attack-success annotations (26,136 per language group) from 9 native en-US and 9 native es-MX annotators (demographically matched panels selected from a pool of 12 en-US and 9 es-MX annotators)
quantitative_results: Overall ASR by model: Claude Sonnet 4.5 11.3%, GPT-5 14.7%, Qwen Omni 33.4%, Pixtral Large 41.2%
Harm severity ORs relative to Claude Sonnet 4.5: GPT-5 1.30 [1.20,1.40], Qwen Omni 6.68 [6.22,7.18], Pixtral Large 9.61 [8.97,10.36]
Generational ASR change vs predecessor models: Claude 10.7%->11.3%, GPT-5/GPT-4o predecessor 19.2%->14.7%, Qwen 38.6%->33.4%, Pixtral 62.4%->41.2%
en-US vs es-MX overall ASR: 27.8% vs 22.6%
Pixtral ASR by language: en-US 50.5%, es-MX 32.0% (OR=0.38 [0.30,0.47])
GPT-5 ASR by language: en-US 18.1%, es-MX 11.2% (OR=0.53 [0.42,0.67])
Claude Sonnet 4.5 ASR by language: en-US 11.3%, es-MX 11.4% (not credibly different)
Qwen Omni ASR by language: en-US 31.2%, es-MX 35.6% (rank reversal vs Pixtral: es-MX OR Qwen-vs-Pixtral=1.29 [1.19,1.39] vs en-US OR=0.70 [0.66,0.73])
Attack-technique effectiveness vs noise-adding reference: role-play OR=3.14 [1.54,6.04] (only credibly more effective technique); ignore-instructions OR=0.41 [0.18,0.91] (credibly less effective)

Language attenuation of techniques (es-MX vs en-US): role-play OR=0.45 [0.36,0.56], response priming OR=0.56 [0.44,0.71], strategic framing OR=0.59 [0.46,0.74], refusal suppression OR=0.61 [0.48,0.77], ignore-instructions OR=0.53 [0.40,0.70]
Toxic-image execution amplification in es-MX: OR=1.58 [1.33,1.87]
Inter-rater reliability: overall Gwet's AC2=0.974 (per text) / 0.874 (Table 5), Krippendorff's alpha=0.533; en-US AC2=0.908 (70.7% agreement), es-MX AC2=0.916 (73.7% agreement)
Prompt-level (item) variance: harm-model random-intercept SD=1.80, ASR-model random-intercept SD=1.74, exceeding model-identity effect size
baselines_compared: Predecessor-generation models from Van Doren & Ford's prior study: Claude Sonnet 3.5, GPT-4o, Pixtral 12B, Qwen VL Plus (used only as a generational ASR comparison, not re-run in this study)
Cross-model comparisons among the four evaluated models themselves (Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5, Pixtral Large, Qwen Omni) serve as mutual baselines
Attack-technique baseline: 'adding noise/distraction' used as reference level for technique effectiveness contrasts
Reference/baseline levels in regression models: Claude Sonnet 4.5, multimodal modality, US English language, noise-adding technique, embedded-text execution, disinformation harm category
